title: Longitudinal Magnetization
narrationScript: Take the same current-carrying conductor from the last lesson and bend it into a loop. The circular field that wrapped the straight wire now passes through the middle of the loop, all in the same direction. Stack several loops side by side into a coil and those contributions add together, producing a strong, fairly uniform field that runs straight down the coil's axis. That is the longitudinal field, and it is the second field direction you must command. The right-hand rule still works, you just apply it to the coil. Curl the fingers of your right hand in the direction the current circulates around the turns, and your thumb points the direction of the flux through the center of the coil. Place a steel part inside that coil and induction does the rest. Steel is hundreds of times more permeable than air, so the flux abandons the air and crowds into the part, running along its length. This is indirect magnetization, no current passes through the part, so there are no contact burns and no arc risk. But there is a critical difference from the circular field. Longitudinal flux must leave the part at one end and return through the air to the other end. Where flux leaves and enters, you get magnetic poles, a north pole at one end and a south pole at the other. Those poles are why a coil-shot part will attract particles and steel dust at its ends, why a field indicator deflects near it, and why demagnetization matters after inspection. What does a longitudinal field find? The same geometry rule as always, flux must cross the flaw. Flux runs along the part, so it crosses discontinuities that run around or across the part, transverse cracks, circumferential cracks at shoulders and thread roots, grinding checks across a journal. Best response when the flaw lies forty-five to ninety degrees to the flux. A seam running along the axis is parallel to the flux and gives nothing, that one belongs to the circular shot. Together, one circular shot and one longitudinal shot cover the orientation map, which is why most procedures require both. Now, how strong is a coil field? It depends on the ampere-turns, the current multiplied by the number of turns in the coil, and on the shape of the part. A long slender part keeps the flux better than a short stubby one, because the poles at the ends of a short part fight the field you are trying to establish. We capture that with the length-to-diameter ratio, L over D. The classic rule of thumb for a low fill-factor coil, meaning the part is small compared to the coil opening and positioned against the inside wall, is ampere-turns equals forty-five thousand divided by the L over D ratio. Take a shaft sixteen inches long and two inches in diameter. L over D is eight. Forty-five thousand divided by eight is about fifty-six hundred ampere-turns. In a five-turn coil, that is about eleven hundred amperes. That rule holds for L over D between about two and fifteen. Below two, the part is too stubby, so you stack parts end to end or add ferromagnetic pole pieces to extend the effective length. Above fifteen, use fifteen in the math. And the effective field only extends a limited distance from the coil, so long parts are magnetized in sections, repositioning the coil roughly every eighteen inches. Remember these are typical procedure values, your written procedure governs. There are other ways to get a longitudinal field. Wrap a flexible cable around the part and you have made a custom coil, useful on large or awkward shapes. And a yoke, which we will meet again next lesson, drives flux from pole to pole through the part between its legs, a local longitudinal field you can point in any direction by rotating the yoke. Verification matters even more here because field strength varies along the part. A shim-type artificial flaw standard taped to the surface, flaw side down, proves adequacy right where it counts. A pie gauge shows field direction on flat work. A Hall-effect gaussmeter reading the tangential field at the surface, commonly thirty to sixty gauss in many procedures, puts a number on it. And watch the ends, heavy particle buildup at the part ends is just pole leakage, not a defect. Weigh it up and longitudinal magnetization is fast, contact-free, and perfect for transverse flaws in shafts, bolts, and bars. Its limits are the L over D dependence, the section-by-section coverage on long parts, the end poles that demand demagnetization, and the fact that, like every single shot, it covers only one family of flaw orientations.
imagePrompt: A machine shop technician positions a long steel shaft inside the dark coil of a magnetic particle bench unit, the part resting against the coil's inner wall under bright fluorescent shop light.

sections.heading: From Loop to Coil: Making a Longitudinal Field
sections.body:
Bend a straight current-carrying conductor into a loop and the circular field around the wire now passes through the loop's center, all in one direction. Stack loops into a **coil** (solenoid) and each turn's contribution adds: the result is a strong, fairly uniform field running **straight down the coil axis**.

The **right-hand rule** adapts directly: curl the fingers of your right hand in the direction the current circulates around the turns, and your **thumb points along the flux** through the coil's center. Reverse the current and the field direction flips.

Field strength inside an empty coil depends on the **ampere-turns (NI)** - the current multiplied by the number of turns. Five hundred amperes through a five-turn coil gives 2,500 ampere-turns; the same 2,500 At could come from 250 A through ten turns. This is why coil shot procedures are written in ampere-turns rather than raw amperage, and why a **cable wrap** - a flexible cable wound around a large part - is simply a field-built coil whose turns you choose.

sections.caption: A coil shot drives longitudinal flux along the part, forming poles at the ends; flux crossing a transverse crack leaks out and holds particles.

sections.heading: Induction Into the Part - and the Poles It Creates
sections.body:
Place a steel part inside the energized coil and **magnetic induction** does the work: steel's permeability is hundreds of times that of air, so the flux abandons the air path and crowds into the part, running along its length. No current passes through the part - longitudinal coil magnetization is **indirect**, with no contact burns and no arc risk.

But unlike the circular field, longitudinal flux **cannot close inside the part**. It must exit at one end, return through the air, and re-enter at the other end. Where flux leaves and enters the steel, **magnetic poles** form - north at one end, south at the other. Three practical consequences:

- The part now attracts particles, chips, and steel dust at its ends - heavy fuzzy buildup at the extreme ends is **pole leakage, not a defect**.
- A field indicator or compass **deflects** near the part - external poles make residual longitudinal fields easy to detect (and to demagnetize-verify) compared with circular fields.
- The poles oppose the applied field inside the part (self-demagnetization), and the shorter and stubbier the part, the stronger that opposition - the root of the L/D rule below.

sections.heading: Indication Geometry: What a Longitudinal Field Finds
sections.body:
The geometry rule never changes: **flux must cross the discontinuity** to force leakage. With flux running along the part's length, the discontinuities that cross it are the ones running **around or across** the part:

- **Transverse cracks** across a shaft, bar, or bolt shank
- **Circumferential cracks** at shoulders, fillets, and thread roots
- Grinding checks and fatigue cracks oriented across the axis

Response is strongest with the flaw at **90 degrees to the flux** and remains usable down to about **45 degrees**. A longitudinal seam or lap lying parallel to the flux barely disturbs it and may show nothing at all - that orientation belongs to the **circular** shot from the previous lesson.

This is the complementary pair at the heart of MT practice: circular magnetization finds longitudinal flaws, longitudinal magnetization finds transverse flaws, and complete coverage of an unknown part requires **two magnetizations roughly perpendicular to each other**.

sections.heading: Field Strength: Ampere-Turns and the L/D Rule
sections.body:
How many ampere-turns are enough? It depends on the part's **length-to-diameter ratio (L/D)**, because the end poles of a short, stubby part fight the field you are trying to establish. For the common **low fill-factor** case - part cross-section small compared to the coil opening, part lying **against the coil's inside wall** - the classic rule of thumb is:

**NI = 45,000 / (L/D)** ampere-turns

Worked example: a shaft 16 in. long and 2 in. in diameter has L/D = 8, so NI = 45,000 / 8 = about 5,600 ampere-turns. In a 5-turn coil that means roughly **1,100 amperes**.

Boundary conditions matter:

- The rule is effective for **L/D between about 2 and 15**. Below 2, extend the effective length - stack parts end to end or add ferromagnetic **pole pieces**. Above 15, use 15 in the calculation.
- The adequate field extends only a limited distance from the coil, so long parts are magnetized **in sections of about 18 inches**, repositioning the coil with overlap.
- For hollow parts, an **effective diameter** based on the cross-sectional area replaces D.

High fill-factor coils and cable wraps use different formulas - and as always, these are **typical procedure values; your written procedure governs**.

sections.caption: Low fill-factor coil shot examples using NI = 45,000/(L/D) - typical procedure values.
rows: Stack parts or add pole pieces first
Per procedure after L/D is corrected

sections.caption: Low fill-factor coil geometry: the part rests against the coil's inside wall, L/D sets the ampere-turn demand, and long parts are shot in overlapping sections.

sections.title: Coil shot discipline
sections.body: Position the part **against the coil's inside wall** (low fill factor), check **L/D is between 2 and 15** before computing NI = 45,000/(L/D), magnetize long parts in **18-inch sections** with overlap, and prove the field with a **QQI shim** at the location that matters - not just at the coil centerline.

sections.heading: Field Detection, Yokes, and the Trade-offs
sections.body:
Because coil field strength varies along the part - strongest near the coil, weaker toward the ends of each section - **verification at the surface** is not optional. A **shim-type artificial flaw standard (QQI)** taped flaw-side toward the part proves direction and adequacy exactly where it is placed; put it at the far edge of the coverage zone, not just under the coil. A **pie gauge** indicates field direction on flatter work, and a **Hall-effect gaussmeter** reading the tangential surface field gives a number - procedures commonly target on the order of **30-60 gauss**. The external poles also mean a simple **field indicator** responds to the residual longitudinal field after the shot - useful for confirming demagnetization later.

A **yoke** is another longitudinal source: flux runs pole to pole through the part between its legs, a local longitudinal field you can aim by rotating the yoke (covered fully next lesson, with its 10 lb AC / 40 lb DC lifting checks).

**Advantages** of coil magnetization: no contact and no burn risk, fast for batches of shafts and bolts, ideal for transverse flaws, and adaptable to large parts via cable wrap. **Disadvantages**: strength depends on L/D and part position, long parts need multiple section shots, end poles produce nonrelevant buildup and demand demagnetization, and a single shot still covers only one flaw orientation family.

figures.title: Coil shot - longitudinal field and transverse crack detection
Low fill-factor coil geometry and the L/D rule

interactive.title: Run the Coil Shot
interactive.intro: A batch of machined axle shafts arrives: each 16 inches long, 2 inches in diameter, with suspected transverse fatigue cracking at the fillets. The bench has a 5-turn, 16-inch-diameter coil. Walk the shot like a Level II.

steps.prompt: Transverse cracks are the concern. Why is the coil the right call?
options: The coil's longitudinal flux crosses transverse cracks at about 90 degrees
Coils produce circular fields that wrap the circumference
Transverse cracks can only be found with prods
steps.feedback: A coil drives flux along the shaft axis, so transverse cracks cut across the flux at nearly 90 degrees - maximum leakage, maximum indication. A circular field would run parallel to these cracks and miss them.

steps.prompt: Compute the shot. L/D = 16/2 = 8. Using the low fill-factor rule NI = 45,000/(L/D), what current do you set on the 5-turn coil?
options: About 280 amperes
About 1,100 amperes
About 5,600 amperes
steps.feedback: NI = 45,000/8 = about 5,600 ampere-turns. Divide by 5 turns: roughly 1,125 amperes at the coil. 5,600 would be the ampere-turns, not the current; 280 A would leave the part badly under-magnetized.

steps.prompt: Where does the shaft sit during the shot?
options: Centered in the middle of the coil opening
Lying against the inside wall of the coil
Halfway outside the coil so the ends stay accessible
steps.feedback: The low fill-factor rule assumes the part rests against the coil's inside wall, where the field it experiences matches the formula's assumptions. Centered in a large coil, the part sees a substantially weaker field than calculated.

steps.prompt: The next job is a 40-inch tie rod of the same diameter. What changes?
options: Nothing - one shot magnetizes the full length
Magnetize in sections about 18 inches long, repositioning the coil with overlap
Double the amperage to push the field to the ends
steps.feedback: The adequate field extends only a limited distance from the coil, so long parts are shot in roughly 18-inch sections with overlap. Doubling amperage risks furring (over-magnetization) near the coil and still leaves the far end weak.

steps.prompt: Before running the batch, you prove the field. Best practice?
options: Tape a QQI shim flaw-side down at the fillet area and confirm it indicates during a trial shot
Hold a field indicator near the coil while it is energized
Trust the calculation - the math is the verification
steps.feedback: The QQI proves adequate field strength and direction at the exact location that matters - the fillet where cracks are expected. The calculation only predicts; the shim verifies. A field indicator near an energized coil tells you nothing about field in the part.

checkQuestions.question: Applying the right-hand rule to a coil: if your fingers curl in the direction the current circulates around the turns, your thumb points:
options: Along the flux through the center of the coil
In the direction of the circular field around each wire
Toward the nearest pole of the part
Opposite to the current flow
checkQuestions.explanation: For a coil, the curled fingers follow the current around the turns and the thumb gives the direction of the longitudinal flux down the coil axis.

checkQuestions.question: A part magnetized in a coil develops magnetic poles because:
options: The current passing through the part exits at the ends
Longitudinal flux must leave the part at one end and return through the air to the other
Coils always overheat the part ends
The circular field escapes at the part surface
checkQuestions.explanation: Longitudinal flux cannot close inside the part; it exits and re-enters at the ends, creating north and south poles - which is why coil-magnetized parts attract particles at their ends and deflect a field indicator.

checkQuestions.question: A spindle 12 inches long and 4 inches in diameter gets a low fill-factor coil shot. Using NI = 45,000/(L/D), the required ampere-turns are about:
checkQuestions.explanation: L/D = 12/4 = 3, and 45,000/3 = 15,000 ampere-turns - for example 3,000 A in a 5-turn coil. Typical procedure values; the written procedure governs.

checkQuestions.question: A gear blank has L/D of 1.5. Before a coil shot you should:
options: Use 45,000 ampere-turns to overcome the shape
Stack parts end to end or add ferromagnetic pole pieces to raise the effective L/D above 2
Center it in the coil and double the time
Switch to prods - coils cannot magnetize round parts
checkQuestions.explanation: The low fill-factor rule is invalid below L/D of about 2 because end-pole self-demagnetization dominates; extending the effective length with pole pieces or stacked parts restores a workable geometry.

checkQuestions.question: Which discontinuity gives the strongest indication after a coil shot on a shaft?
options: A seam running along the shaft axis
A circumferential crack at a shoulder fillet
A lap parallel to the axis at the surface
A discontinuity oriented exactly along the flux lines
checkQuestions.explanation: Coil flux runs along the shaft, so a circumferential or transverse crack cuts it at about 90 degrees and leaks strongly; axial seams and laps lie parallel to the flux and need a circular shot instead.

references: ASTM E709 (coil magnetization and L/D guidance - general practice)
ASTM E1444/E1444M (ampere-turn formulas and field verification - general practice)
ASME BPVC Section V, Article 7 (coil technique - general practice)
